draft.introduction: The Great Law of Peace (Gayanashagowa) is the oral constitution of the Haudenosaunee (Iroquois) Confederacy, established centuries before European contact. It is one of the oldest continuously operating democratic frameworks in the world.

translation.section: The Planting of the Tree of Peace
translation.text: I am Dekanawidah, and with the Five Nations confederate lords I plant the Tree of Great Peace. I plant it in your territory, Adodarhoh, and the Onondaga nation—in the territory of you who are firekeepers. I name the tree the Tree of the Great Long Leaves. Under the shade of this Tree of Great Peace we spread the soft white feathery down of the globe thistle as seats for you, Adodarhoh, and your cousin lords. Roots have spread out from the Tree of Great Peace, one to the north, one to the east, one to the south, and one to the west. The name of these roots is the Great White Roots, and their nature is peace and strength.

translator_notes.ref: The Planting of the Tree of Peace
translator_notes.note: The Tree of Peace is both a literal white pine and a symbol of the confederacy's constitution. The roots extending in four directions invite any nation to follow them to their source and join the peace. Several historians have argued that the Great Law influenced the framers of the United States Constitution, particularly its federal structure. The U.S. Congress formally acknowledged this influence in 1988.
